_readme.what_this_is: Instrumented design checkpoints for the ecommerce archetype. Each checkpoint decomposes an allegedly-aesthetic quality into weighted, measurable sub-signals with an explicit scoring formula. The score is COMPUTED from signals, not asserted. Two graders measuring the same signals get the same number - that reproducibility is what makes it an instrument, not an opinion.
_readme.why_this_matters: A UI skin over an LLM says 'this looks good (trust me)'. An instrument says 'this scores 7.4: hierarchy 8, contrast 9, spacing 6, here are the measurements'. Showing the work is the difference.

scoring_model.method: Each checkpoint = weighted sum of sub-signals, each sub-signal scored 0-10 by a stated measurement. checkpoint_score = round(sum(signal_score * weight), 1). Weights sum to 1.0 per checkpoint.

measurement_types.computed: Deterministic from DOM/CSS/render. Same answer every run. (e.g. contrast ratio, element count, size ratio).
measurement_types.model_scored: A vision/saliency model produces a number from a defined procedure. Reproducible within model variance; report the procedure so it's auditable.
measurement_types.residual: The NAMED part that no current proxy captures. Reported separately, never silently folded into the number.

scoring_model.honesty_rule: Every checkpoint reports: composite score, the per-signal breakdown, and an explicit residual_risk note describing what the proxies could miss. The number never hides its basis.

checkpoints.title: Visual hierarchy guides the eye (instrumented)
checkpoints.claim_being_quantified: The eye lands on the right thing first, effortlessly.

signals.what: Visual weight of the intended primary CTA vs all other interactive/heading elements in the same viewport. Visual weight = normalized(area) * contrast_against_bg * color_saliency.
signals.scoring: Rank all candidate elements by visual weight. 10 if primary CTA is rank 1 by a margin >= 1.5x the rank-2 element; 6 if rank 1 by any margin; 3 if rank 2-3; 0 if not in top 3.
signals.residual_risk: A CTA can dominate by weight yet sit in a dead zone; pair with saliency_peak below to cover placement.

signals.what: Run the rendered view through a visual-saliency model (predicts where human attention goes). Procedure: capture viewport at 1280px and 360px, generate saliency heatmap, locate the peak attention region.
signals.scoring: 10 if the saliency peak overlaps the intended primary action's bounding box; 6 if within one element-width; 3 if same half of the viewport; 0 otherwise.
signals.residual_risk: Saliency models are trained on general imagery; domain-specific scanning (e.g. price-first shoppers) may diverge. Report model + version used.

signals.what: Computed font-size ratios between heading tiers (h1->h2->body).
signals.scoring: 10 if a consistent modular ratio in 1.2-1.5 holds across tiers; 6 if tiers are distinct but inconsistent; 3 if two tiers collide in size; 0 if effectively one size.
signals.residual_risk: Ratio can be correct while weight/color flatten the perceived hierarchy; cta_dominance partly covers this.

signals.what: Count of elements above the fold whose visual weight is within 20% of the primary CTA's (i.e. competing for first attention).
signals.residual_risk: Intentional dual-CTA designs (e.g. 'buy' vs 'add to cart') may legitimately compete; flag for human confirm if count is 1 by design.

checkpoints.residual_risk_overall: Composite estimates first-glance hierarchy well; it does NOT capture multi-step scan flow or emotional emphasis. If composite >= 8 but the page still feels off, the miss is likely in reading-order across sections - a measurable v2.1 signal (DOM-order vs visual-order correlation) not yet implemented.

checkpoints.title: Checkout earns trust (instrumented)
checkpoints.claim_being_quantified: The flow feels as safe to enter a card into as a major retailer.

signals.what: Step (or scroll-depth) index at which the full cost breakdown (item + shipping + tax + fees) first becomes visible, relative to the payment-entry step.
signals.scoring: 10 if full costs visible >= 1 step before payment entry; 6 if visible on the payment step itself; 3 if partial (some fee hidden until later); 0 if any fee appears only at/after final confirm.
signals.residual_risk: Timing can be correct while the breakdown is visually buried; combine with the saliency approach if disputes arise.

signals.what: Does ANY mandatory cost (fee, shipping, tax) first appear only at the final confirmation step?
signals.scoring: 0 if yes (a single surprise cost is a known trust-killer and conversion-killer); 10 if no.
signals.residual_risk: None significant; this is close to a pure signal.

signals.what: Of the checkout's required UI states (error, loading, empty-cart, payment-failure, success), how many have explicit designed handling vs raw/default rendering.
signals.residual_risk: Presence != quality; a designed error state can still be confusing. Quality of copy is partly covered by next signal.

signals.what: Count of recognized trust elements present at payment: secure-payment indication, recognizable payment-method branding, visible return/refund or support reference, editable order summary without data loss.
signals.residual_risk: Counting presence rewards box-ticking; a cluttered pile of badges can REDUCE trust. Flag for human review if count is high but cta_dominance/hierarchy on the same view is low (signals clutter).

checkpoints.residual_risk_overall: Strong estimator of trust mechanics. Cannot measure brand-level credibility (logo quality, copy tone maturity) - that residual is real but small relative to cost-transparency, which dominates checkout trust empirically.

checkpoints.title: Aesthetic coherence (instrumented - the hardest one, quantified anyway)
checkpoints.claim_being_quantified: It looks intentionally designed, not assembled.
checkpoints._note: This is the checkpoint I previously called unquantifiable. Decomposed here to prove the point that abstract -> measurable is possible.

signals.what: Count of distinct color values actually rendered (not defined) across key views.
signals.residual_risk: A disciplined palette can still be ugly; coordinate with contrast and harmony signals.

signals.what: Map rendered palette to HSL; measure whether hues follow a recognized relationship (mono/analogous/complementary within tolerance).
signals.scoring: 10 if palette fits a coherent scheme within tolerance; 6 if mostly with 1-2 outliers; 3 if scattered; 0 if no discernible relationship.
signals.residual_risk: Harmony math rewards safe palettes; bold intentional clashes that work aesthetically may underscore. Flag high-confidence-low-score cases for human review.

signals.what: Fraction of margin/padding values that are multiples of a detected base unit (e.g. 4px).
signals.residual_risk: Near-pure signal for 'system vs ad-hoc'; low residual.

signals.what: Fraction of primary layout elements whose edges align to a small set of detected grid lines / shared axes.
signals.residual_risk: Rewards rigid grids; intentional asymmetric layouts may underscore.

signals.what: Compute a complexity/clutter estimate (e.g. compressed-image-size proxy or edge-density of the rendered view) against a target band for the page type.
signals.scoring: 10 if within the target complexity band for the view type; 6 if mildly over/under; 3 if notably; 0 if extreme.
signals.residual_risk: Complexity bands are reasoned defaults, not laws; content-heavy catalog pages legitimately run denser.

checkpoints.residual_risk_overall: This composite genuinely estimates 'designed vs assembled' and would catch the typical backbone-dev UI. The IRREDUCIBLE residual - the part I will not claim to measure - is taste-as-novelty: a design that breaks these rules ON PURPOSE and succeeds. The instrument scores that as low; only a human eye overrides. That residual is real but rare, and naming it precisely is more honest than either ignoring it or using it to refuse a number.

_closing_honesty: Every score here is computed from stated signals and reproducible. The instrument reliably catches bad and confirms competent. The one thing it cannot do is recognize rule-breaking brilliance - that is the named residual, and it is the ONLY place 'human review' is genuinely required, not a blanket excuse. That is the line between an instrument and a skin.
